region_of_origin: Egypt / Mediterranean (widely consumed globally; served in Singapore by Starbucks)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 240 ml (one cup) of brewed chamomile tea, equivalent to one Starbucks tall-size cup as served at the outlet.

nutrient_source.title: Chamomile tea, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173228)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed chamomile tea (unsweetened) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent plain brewed chamomile tea without added sugar, consistent with the HPB no-added-sugar endorsement noted in the product description.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-nutritive beverages — plain herbal tea convention; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chamomile tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates (carbohydrate_g = 0.0 per 100 g per USDA SR Legacy). No direct GI measurement exists for chamomile tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. By established convention for non-nutritive herbal infusions (analogous to plain green tea and black tea, GI ≈ 0–1), glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate content is effectively zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (1 mg per 100 g); no added sugar; HPB no-added-sugar endorsement
hypertension.guidance: Chamomile tea is an appropriate beverage choice for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content and absence of added sugars; it may be consumed freely as part of a low-sodium dietary pattern, provided no high-sodium additives are included.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; no added sugar; glycemic load of 0.0 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chamomile tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates and has a glycemic load of zero per serving, making it a safe and suitable beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar or sweeteners; the HPB no-added-sugar endorsement supports its use in a diabetes-friendly diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat; zero cholesterol; no added sugar; antioxidant flavonoid content (apigenin) from chamomile flowers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chamomile tea contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and no added sugar, and its flavonoid content (notably apigenin) has been associated with mild anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties in peer-reviewed literature, supporting its suitability as a heart-friendly beverage for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chamomile Tea is a caffeine-free herbal infusion made by steeping dried chamomile flowers (Matricaria chamomilla) and complementary botanicals such as rose petals in hot water. The beverage has a distinctive floral bouquet with a mild apple-like sweetness and subtle toasty undertones, making it one of the most widely consumed herbal teas globally. It contains no tea leaves (Camellia sinensis) and is therefore naturally caffeine-free. The version served at Starbucks Singapore (One Fullerton) is a blended herbal tisane endorsed by the Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore under its no-added-sugar programme, confirming the absence of added sugars or sweeteners. Nutritionally, chamomile tea is essentially calorie-free at approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml, with negligible protein, fat, and carbohydrate content. It provides trace amounts of potassium (9 mg/100 g), calcium (2 mg/100 g), and iron (0.08 mg/100 g). Its glycemic index is effectively zero (set to 1 by convention for non-nutritive beverages), and its glycemic load per serving is 0.0. Chamomile tea is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its negligible sodium, zero glycemic carbohydrates, and absence of saturated fat. The flavonoid apigenin present in chamomile has been studied for mild anti-inflammatory, anxiolytic, and antioxidant properties. It is vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements. In Singapore, it is commonly consumed as a relaxing evening beverage or as a caffeine-free alternative to coffee and black tea.
common_user_queries: Is chamomile tea good for diabetics?
Is chamomile tea suitable for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of chamomile tea?
What are the health benefits of chamomile tea?
Is chamomile tea vegan and halal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173228) for brewed chamomile tea, which is an exact match to the food. GI is set by established convention for non-nutritive herbal infusions with zero carbohydrate content, documented with peer-reviewed GI table reference. HPB no-added-sugar endorsement is consistent with the product description. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-referenced.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g (not zero): brewed chamomile tea contains a trace of energy per USDA SR Legacy; this is not a zero-energy food but is effectively negligible. 2. protein_g is naturally zero: brewed chamomile tea contains no protein as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173228). 3. fat_g is naturally zero: brewed chamomile tea contains no fat as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: brewed chamomile tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. sugar_g is naturally zero: no added sugar and no intrinsic sugar detected per USDA SR Legacy. 6. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: fibre is not present in the brewed liquid infusion per USDA SR Legacy. 7. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for non-nutritive herbal infusions. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0.0. 8. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for brewed chamomile tea. 9. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 10. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 11. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 12. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 13. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 14. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 15. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 16. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 17. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 18. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 19. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this food is served in Singapore and the beverage category requires Singaporean multilingual coverage per G6. 20. The cuisine tag in the input was 'Coffee' (a field survey platform category tag); the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' consistent with the cuisine_tags array in the input. 21. Price data (SGD 6.20) preserved from field survey source for reference but not included in the RESULT template as it is not a template field. 22. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP and SNOMED CT code 227313005 are used as best available defensible codes for chamomile tea; reviewers should verify these codes against current ontology releases.
